refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up / total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ 0.999. Requires instrumentation of link health across all network segments.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: Provide bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ [TBD] ms
FR-NGHE-010.2: Provide bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ [TBD] ms
FR-NGHE-010.3: Detect link degradation or loss and trigger failsafe response within [TBD] ms
FR-NGHE-010.4: Monitor and report link availability metrics in real-time to operator and safety manager

candidate_dps: DP-A: Redundant wireless network paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; dual-modem architecture on vehicle and tele-operator station
DP-B: Single high-reliability wireless link with error correction and retransmission; single-modem architecture with robust protocol
DP-C: Hybrid wired-wireless: tethered fiber optic cable for primary link, wireless backup; vehicle-mounted cable reel with slip-ring interface
DP-D: Mesh network topology with multi-hop relay nodes; distributed routing to maximize path diversity and resilience
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Redundant wireless network paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; dual-modem architecture on vehicle and tele-operator station

potential_sources: Coupling between uplink and downlink availability: both must be operational simultaneously; DP-A mitigates by independent redundancy on each path
Coupling between vehicle modem and tele-operator modem: both must support same protocol; addressed by specification of common interface standard
Coupling between failover logic and safety response: failover must not introduce unsafe transient states; requires coordination with Safety Services
coupling_check.notes: DP-A decouples primary and backup paths, allowing independent optimization. Potential weak coupling remains between command latency and telemetry latency; recommend separate SLAs per sub-FR.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Dual-modem hardware is available and cost-effective for vehicle and tele-operator station
Assumption: Wireless spectrum (frequency bands) is available and licensed for the operational geography
Risk: Failover switching time may introduce brief link interruption; must be < 100 ms to avoid safety impact
Risk: Correlated failure mode (e.g., both modems affected by same RF interference) could defeat redundancy; requires spatial/frequency separation analysis
Risk: Increased power consumption and thermal load from dual modems; vehicle battery endurance may be impacted
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly specify: (1) modem separation strategy (spatial, frequency, or time-domain), (2) failover detection and switching logic (heartbeat interval, threshold), (3) protocol for graceful degradation if one path is partially available, (4) power budget allocation for dual-modem operation.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the bidirectional wireless communication infrastructure, including dual-modem hardware on vehicle and tele-operator station, failover logic, link health monitoring, and real-time availability reporting. Interfaces with Vehicle for modem integration and with Safety Services for failsafe response coordination.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (steering, throttle, bucket control, etc.) from tele-operator station; encapsulated in command packets with sequence numbers and checksums

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, equipment status) from vehicle to tele-operator station and monitoring systems; includes link quality metrics (RSSI, packet loss, latency)

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and failsafe directives from Safety Services; transmits link availability status and degradation warnings to Safety Services for real-time risk assessment

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts the underlying wireless network infrastructure (modems, antennas, RF propagation); provides logical link state (up/down) and quality metrics to Teleoperation module

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, dual-modem architecture, and failover control. Minimal diffusion: only Network Link abstraction and SafetyAlert coordination cross boundaries. High cohesion within module.

Vehicle could host modem hardware and failover logic, but this couples communication infrastructure tightly to vehicle platform. Would require Vehicle to own network protocol details, reducing modularity. Not recommended as primary owner.

Safety Services should monitor link availability and trigger failsafe responses, but should not own the communication infrastructure itself. Placing DP-A here would diffuse network engineering concerns across safety logic. Recommend Safety Services as consumer of link status, not owner.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but too coarse-grained to own detailed link availability logic. Would result in high diffusion across sub-modules. Recommend as integration point only.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns dual-modem architecture, failover logic, link health monitoring, and availability reporting. Vehicle provides physical modem mounting and power; Safety Services consumes link status for failsafe decisions. This assignment maximizes cohesion and minimizes diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become too large if it also owns tele-operator station UI and command encoding; recommend clear sub-boundaries (e.g., Teleoperation.LinkManagement vs. Teleoperation.CommandInterface)

Risk: Vehicle modem integration requires tight coupling with Vehicle electrical architecture; recommend Vehicle expose a standardized modem power/data interface to decouple Teleoperation from vehicle-specific details
Risk: SafetyAlert interface requires real-time link status updates; latency in SafetyAlert path could delay failsafe response; recommend dedicated high-priority messaging channel
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine IM assignment to explicitly define: (1) Teleoperation.LinkManagement sub-module (owns dual-modem, failover, monitoring), (2) Vehicle.ModemInterface sub-module (owns physical integration, power management), (3) SafetyAlert contract (link status update frequency, latency SLA), (4) Network Link abstraction layer (hides RF details from Teleoperation logic).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (redundant wireless paths with automatic failover) directly addresses FR requirement for ≥0.999 availability. Dual-modem architecture with independent paths provides the logical mechanism to achieve high availability. Coupling analysis confirms decoupling of primary/backup paths. Assumptions and risks are identified and manageable with detailed design.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: Failover logic may need to coordinate with Vehicle control logic (e.g., safe stop if link lost); recommend clear interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle
Diffusion risk: Modem hardware selection and RF design may be constrained by Vehicle form factor and power budget; recommend early Vehicle-Teleoperation co-design
Diffusion risk: Link availability metrics must be reported to multiple consumers (tele-operator, Safety Manager, System Engineer); recommend publish-subscribe pattern to avoid point-to-point coupling
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module containment score of 0.95 is strong. Primary external dependencies are Network Link (abstraction) and SafetyAlert (coordination). Vehicle integration is via standardized modem interface. Recommend Teleoperation own all link management logic; Vehicle own only physical modem mounting and power delivery.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: continuous link state monitoring during evaluation period, automated availability calculation, real-time reporting to test infrastructure. Recommend instrumentation of both primary and backup paths to diagnose failover behavior.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns link availability delivery; Vehicle provides modem integration; Safety Services consumes link status for failsafe decisions. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and traceable to sub-FRs.

recommended_revisions: Refine sub-FR latency targets (FR-NGHE-010.1 and 010.2) to quantify acceptable command and telemetry delays; recommend ≤ 100 ms for safety-critical operations
Refine sub-FR failsafe response time (FR-NGHE-010.3) to specify maximum detection and failsafe action latency; recommend ≤ 50 ms
Refine sub-FR monitoring (FR-NGHE-010.4) to specify reporting frequency and granularity (e.g., per-second availability updates, per-minute aggregates)
Add explicit DP revision to specify modem separation strategy (frequency bands, spatial separation, time-domain multiplexing) to mitigate correlated failure risk

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs addressing command uplink, telemetry downlink, failsafe detection, and monitoring. Each sub-FR requires detailed design and verification. DP-A (redundant wireless paths) is sound and well-scoped to Teleoperation module, but sub-FR decomposition is necessary to allocate design responsibility and verify each aspect independently. Recommend proceeding to next decomposition level for each sub-FR.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: Provide bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ [TBD] ms and packet delivery rate ≥ 99.9%
FR-NGHE-010.2: Provide bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ [TBD] ms and packet delivery rate ≥ 99.9%
FR-NGHE-010.3: Detect link degradation or loss and trigger failsafe response (safe stop, operator alert) within [TBD] ms
FR-NGHE-010.4: Monitor and report link availability metrics (uptime %, packet loss %, latency percentiles) in real-time to operator and safety manager

revise.why: Refine sub-FR latency and packet delivery targets based on safety analysis and operator feedback. Current sub-FRs use [TBD] placeholders; recommend Safety Manager and Tele-operator input to finalize acceptable latency and loss thresholds.

assumptions: Dual-modem hardware is cost-effective and available for vehicle and tele-operator station
Wireless spectrum is available and licensed for operational geography
Failover switching time can be achieved in < 100 ms without safety impact
Spatial or frequency separation of modems can mitigate correlated failure risk
Vehicle electrical architecture can support dual-modem power and data integration
Safety Services can respond to link status updates with failsafe actions in < 50 ms
open_risks: Correlated failure mode: both modems affected by same RF interference or environmental condition; mitigation: detailed RF propagation analysis and modem separation strategy
Failover transient: brief link interruption during modem switching; mitigation: operator training and failsafe response design
Power budget: dual modems increase vehicle power consumption; mitigation: battery endurance analysis and possible solar/charging infrastructure
Latency coupling: command uplink and telemetry downlink latencies must be balanced for operator situational awareness; mitigation: separate SLAs and real-time latency monitoring
Spectrum interference: unlicensed or shared spectrum may experience interference; mitigation: frequency hopping or adaptive modulation
evidence_needed: RF propagation model for operational site(s); confirm dual-modem separation strategy is feasible
Modem hardware specifications and failover switching time measurements
Vehicle electrical architecture review; confirm power and data integration points
Safety analysis (FMEA or FTA) for link loss scenarios; confirm failsafe response adequacy
Operator feedback on acceptable latency and link quality thresholds
Evaluation period definition and measurement methodology (continuous monitoring, data logging, availability calculation)
